It's hard to say. I tried for quite a while without success to get the StreamReader/Writer working but with little success. I don't know it as well though so it's probable I got something messed up due to lack of familiarity. Well it isn't that I didn't get it working, it's that it broke the tests in horrible ways and I couldn't figure out [quickly] how to go about rewriting things so that they worked as expected.

Also @markrwilliams  with regards to the len property, you're right. There I'm returning the actual file size in one form or another. The variable byte width of utf-8 of course does mean exactly what you imply. I'm wondering though if this really is the best approach.

I've gone the route of using len to indicate actual size, but implemented seek, tell, etc... in a way that moves across the code points rather than individual bytes (so that you couldn't seek to a point in the middle of a character so to speak).

Maybe the smart approach here is to make it work as you suggest as that more closely matches what StringIO does.

I'm getting the impression that the new implementation treats imports and requirements the same. Both are converted to a single dotted name. So there's no room for:

- Trying to import something to figure out which requirement name would match.
- A package providing multiple top-level paths (like some zope "meta-packages" do).

So I think something a bit more explicit is needed here. A distinction between imports (which are really dotted names) and packages/requirements, which might have to be handled a little bit special.

Ok this is crazy. =) Now that I see exactly what you're trying to do, I can offer some advice.

Firstly, saving a full image for each merge is a huge amount of storage. Given how easy it is to generate a label image from a label remapping (`label_map[labels]`), you could *just* save the `label_map`s.

Secondly, only the label maps for `src` and `dst` are changing at each iteration. Indeed, only the labels in `new_id` need to be updated in the label map; they include all the labels in `src` and `dst`. Instead of making a new `label_map` at each iteration, copy the existing map and update just the labels in `new_id`.

Thirdly, more generally you are making a *merge tree*, and the tree contains all the information to make these maps. I did this in my `viridis` "library" (in quotes because it's so simple... More like a bookshelf, really. =P). https://github.com/jni/viridis/blob/master/viridis/tree.py Look at the `get_map` function. All or parts of it could be used here.

@alfredodeza yeah, good point.  An excellent area to clean up.  I took the multiple asserts directly from the existing tests for ``--help``, e.g. https://github.com/ceph/ceph-deploy/blob/master/ceph_deploy/tests/test_cli_install.py#L11-L13

So yes, Yum automatically updates packages, and DNF does not.

Doing the following on CentOS 7:

```
ceph-deploy install --dev next cent701
ceph-deploy install --dev master cent701
```

does an upgrade, even though the underlying commands are just `yum -y install ceph ceph-radosgw` in each case.

Adding `--best` seems to recreate this behavior.  Of course DNF is new and there may be consequences to `--best` we do not understand yet.  The docs mention that you are more likely to get into dependency resolution problems because you are telling it to always use the newer version of the package, even if it can't resolve the deps.

If that happens, that probably means there's a problem with the packages, IMO.  We'll see!

> > +
> > +    Args:
> > +      file_list: file_list: list of dicts with the file name.
> > +      destination_file: Path to the destination file.
> > +      content_type: Content type for the destination file.
> > +      retry_params: An api_utils.RetryParams for this call to GCS. If None,
> > +      the default one is used.
> > +    _account_id: Internal-use only.
> > +    """
> > +    xml = ''
> > +    for meta_data in file_list:
> > +      xml_meta = ""
> > +      for meta in meta_data:
> > +        xml_meta += "<%s>%s</%s>" % (meta, meta_data[meta], meta)
> > +      xml += '<Component>%s</Component>' % xml_meta
> > +    xml = '<ComposeRequest>%s</ComposeRequest>' % xml
>
> How is it not efficient. Any recommendations?
>
In general a + b + c would be better than (a + "%s" + c) % b  (think from
allocated bytes and byte iteration perspective).
So, in your case plain initialization xml_meta with "<Component>" and
adding "</<Component>" after iteration and doing
the same for xml would be way more efficient (and without doing anything
special).
If you really want to be efficient you should consider (cStringIO or join)

@baroquebobcat A use case that transitive exports might be useful is:
say we have 4 targets A B C and D. C extends D, B extends C, and A uses B.
In that case, if exports is transitive, then C exports D, B exports C will be enough.
But if exports is not transitive, then B has to exports both C and D. Since B only has C in its dependencies list, exporting D is a little unexpected.

The doc for file objects says:

Read at most size bytes from the file (less if the read hits EOF before obtaining size bytes). If the size argument is negative or omitted, read all data until EOF is reached. The bytes are returned as a string object. An empty string is returned when EOF is encountered immediately. (For certain files, like ttys, it makes sense to continue reading after an EOF is hit.) **Note that this method may call the underlying C function fread() more than once in an effort to acquire as close to size bytes as possible.** Also note that when in non-blocking mode, less data than was requested may be returned, even if no size parameter was given.
